name: Authentication
shortDescription: A complete authentication system: JWT, OAuth 2.0, TOTP (2FA), Magic Link, and biometrics via WebAuthn.
description: Interactive demo of the main modern authentication mechanisms: JWT, refresh tokens, OAuth, two-factor authentication with TOTP, passwordless login via Magic Link, and biometrics with WebAuthn/Passkeys.
longDescription: This feature demonstrates the five authentication patterns found in most modern applications. JWT with a short-lived access token and a refresh token in an HttpOnly cookie with automatic rotation; OAuth 2.0 via NextAuth with a Google provider; TOTP with a scannable QR Code and backup codes; passwordless Magic Link over email via Resend; and WebAuthn/Passkeys for biometric login via FIDO2. Every sub-screen is interactive — you watch the token being generated, the real QR Code, the link being sent, and your device's own biometrics being used to authenticate.
tags: JWT, OAuth 2.0, 2FA, TOTP, Magic Link, WebAuthn, Passkeys, FIDO2, NextAuth, Refresh Token, HttpOnly Cookie, CSRF, Security
architecture: Next.js API Routes as the backend, short-lived access token + refresh token in an HttpOnly cookie with automatic rotation, OAuth Authorization Code Flow, TOTP via RFC 6238, WebAuthn/FIDO2 with asymmetric keys
technologies: NextAuth.js, jsonwebtoken, otplib, Resend, WebAuthn API, Next.js API Routes, TypeScript
concepts: Access + Refresh Token, Token Rotation, OAuth 2.0, TOTP (RFC 6238), Magic Link, WebAuthn/Passkeys (FIDO2), HttpOnly cookies vs localStorage, CSRF protection
commonCredentialsHint: Data returned by the server after authentication
commonLoginToSeeCredentials: Log in to see your credentials here.
commonLoginToSeeDecodedToken: Log in to see the decoded token here.
commonSignatureHint: The signature can't be decoded — it's a cryptographic hash that guarantees the token's integrity.
jwtDesc1: JWT (JSON Web Token) is a token format used for authentication and secure information exchange between applications. It works as a digital credential proving a user has already logged in successfully.
jwtDesc2: After login, the server generates a token with the user's information and sends it to the client. On subsequent requests, the client includes that token in the Authorization header to prove its identity — without the server needing to keep a session in memory.
jwtPartsIntro: A JWT is made up of three parts:
jwtPartSignature: Signature — guarantees the token hasn't been tampered with.
jwtDesc3: JWTs are widely used in modern APIs and web and mobile applications, especially in REST and microservices architectures.
jwtRefreshTitle: Refresh token in an HttpOnly cookie
jwtRefreshDesc1: This application's refresh token lives in an HttpOnly cookie — a special cookie the browser sends automatically to the server, but that JavaScript can't read, not even via document.cookie. That removes the main XSS attack vector: even if a malicious script gets injected into the page, it can't steal the refresh token.
jwtRefreshDesc2: The access token, being short-lived (5 minutes), lives in localStorage. Its short lifespan already limits the blast radius of a leak — within minutes it expires and becomes useless.
jwtRotationDesc1: Every time the refresh token is used, the old token is immediately deleted from the database and a new one is generated and stored. This is called automatic rotation.
jwtRotationDesc2: In practice: if someone steals the refresh token and tries to use it after you already have, the server rejects it — that token no longer exists in the database. Any duplicate-use attempt ends the session and forces a new login.
jwtDemoWarning: Heads up: the information entered in these forms is only used to demo JWT registration and authentication. Data is stored in a cloud database and removed by periodic cleanup jobs — none of it is used for any other purpose.
jwtHowToUseDesc: Run the full flow in two steps: create a user and password in the registration form below, then authenticate in the form next to it.
jwtStep1Desc: Fill in the form below with your name, email, and any password that meets the minimum requirements!
jwtNamePlaceholder: Enter your name, or any name at all!
jwtRegisterEmailPlaceholder: Enter your email, or any email at all!
jwtPasswordPlaceholder: Minimum 4 characters: a letter, a number, and a symbol
jwtRegisterSuccess: User created successfully! Now log in.
jwtRegisterErrorEmailTaken: This email is already registered. Try another email or move on to the login form.
jwtRegisterErrorGeneric: Oops, something didn't go as planned. Please try again later.
jwtStep2Desc: Enter your login details so we can authenticate you and issue the JWT!
jwtAuthSuccess: Authenticated! Access token saved to localStorage. Refresh token stored in an HttpOnly cookie.
jwtAuthErrorGeneric: Oops, something didn't go as planned. Please try again later.
jwtValidationPasswordLetter: Must contain at least one letter
jwtValidationPasswordNumber: Must contain at least one number
jwtValidationPasswordSymbol: Must contain at least one special character
jwtSessionRefreshingDesc: The refresh token is being used to obtain a new access token without needing to log in again.
jwtSessionExpiredTitle: Access token expired! Waiting for automatic renewal...
jwtSessionExpiredDesc: The system is using the refresh token to automatically renew your session.
jwtSessionExpiresIn: Your access token is stored in the browser's localStorage and expires in {seconds, plural, one {# second} other {# seconds}}.
jwtRefreshAccessTokenDesc: Sent with every protected request in the Authorization header. Its short lifespan limits the impact of a leak.
jwtRefreshCookieTitle: Refresh Token in an HttpOnly Cookie (7 days)
jwtRefreshCookieDesc: The refresh token lives in an HttpOnly cookie — the browser sends it automatically to the server on every call to /api/jwt/refresh, but no JavaScript code can read it, not even via document.cookie. That shields the token from XSS attacks.
jwtRefreshRotationDesc: On every renewal, the old token is deleted from the database and a new one is generated and saved. If someone steals the token and tries to use it after you already have, the server rejects it — that token no longer exists in the database. This catches duplicate use and ends the compromised session.
jwtSilentRefreshDesc: When you reload the page (F5), the app silently tries to restore your session using the HttpOnly cookie — without asking you to log in again. If the cookie is still valid, the session is restored within seconds, transparently to you.
jwtCountdownDesc: The access token is about to expire. The app will detect this automatically, call /api/jwt/refresh using the cookie, and a new pair of tokens will be issued — you'll see it happen in real time.
magicLinkDesc1: Magic Link is a passwordless authentication method. Instead of asking for a password, the app emails a link that automatically authenticates the user when clicked. It's the same idea as "forgot my password" — but used as the actual login method, not as recovery.
magicLinkDesc2: The user never has to create or remember a password. They prove who they are simply by having access to the inbox of the email they provided.
magicLinkWhyIntro: Passwords are the weakest link in authentication:
magicLinkWhyItem1: Users reuse passwords across services — one leak compromises everything.
magicLinkWhyItem2: Weak passwords dominate: "123456", "password", a kid's name plus a birth year.
magicLinkWhyItem3: Hashed password databases (bcrypt, argon2) still have to be maintained and protected.
magicLinkWhyItem4: Phishing tricks users into typing passwords into fake sites.
magicLinkHowDesc: Magic Link removes all of that. There's no password to leak, reuse, or forget. Security shifts to email — which is already the "recovery factor" for practically every service. Magic Link just makes that the official login method.
magicLinkFlowMagicStep3: You click the link you received.
magicLinkDemoWarning: Heads up: the email you enter below is only used to demo sending and validating the Magic Link. Data is stored in a cloud database and removed by periodic cleanup jobs — none of it is used for any other purpose.
magicLinkDemoHint: The demo is as simple as a walk in the park
magicLinkStep1Desc: Enter the same email you used to log in in the Step 2 — Authentication section of the JWT (JSON Web Token) module.
magicLinkEmailFieldLabel: Email to receive the magic link
magicLinkSuccessDesc: Check your inbox or spam folder. An email was sent for you to log in!
magicLinkReplayWarning: Heads up: since it's a one-time token, the credentials and token below only show up the first time the link is opened. If you refresh the page (F5) or open the same link again, the token will already be consumed and the information disappears — that's the replay protection explained above kicking in. To see it again, request a new Magic Link.
magicLinkExpiredOrUsed: This link has already been used or has expired. Request a new Magic Link above.
magicLinkLoginToSeeCredentials: Log in to see your credentials here.
magicLinkLoginToSeeToken: Log in to see the decoded token here.
magicLinkTokenPanelHint: Below you'll see some information about the token
oauthDesc1: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization protocol that lets a user authenticate with an account they already have on another service (Google, GitHub, etc.) without sharing their password with your application.
oauthDesc2: Google confirms the identity and hands over only the information the user authorized.
oauthVersionCompareTitle: OAuth 1.0 vs OAuth 2.0 — what's the difference?
oauthVersionCompareIntro: OAuth didn't start at version 2.0. Version 1.0 had existed since 2007 with a completely different approach to security. Understanding that evolution helps explain why 2.0 was adopted universally.
oauthV1Item1: Security via cryptographic signatures on every request. The client had to sign each call with HMAC-SHA1 using secret keys — with no HTTPS requirement.
oauthV1Item2: Complex to implement: required computing the signature by hand, sorting parameters, and generating a nonce and timestamp on every request.
oauthV1Item3: No native support for refresh tokens — access tokens didn't expire easily and required manual re-authentication.
oauthV1Item4: A single fixed flow. No support for mobile apps, SPAs, or server-to-server integrations with different strategies.
oauthV2Item1: Security via mandatory HTTPS/TLS. Encryption lives at the transport layer — no need to sign every request by hand.
oauthV2Item2: Much simpler to implement. Tokens are Bearer tokens — just send them in the Authorization header.
oauthV2Item3: Native support for access token + refresh token with short expiration and silent renewal without a new login.
oauthV2Item4: Multiple flows (grant types) for different contexts: Authorization Code for web apps, Client Credentials for server-to-server, Device Flow for TVs and CLIs.
oauthWhyRetiredDesc: Per-request cryptographic signing was secure, but extremely hard to implement correctly — small parameter-ordering or timezone mistakes would silently break everything. OAuth 2.0 shifted that responsibility to HTTPS, which is already mandatory in any modern application, making the protocol simple enough for any developer to implement without errors.
oauthCredentialsTitle: The credentials — what each one is
oauthCredentialsIntro: Before the flow starts, you register your application in the Google Cloud Console and receive credentials. Here's what each one is and who's responsible for it:
oauthClientIdDesc: Your application's public identifier — think of it as the app's tax ID with Google. It shows up in the redirect URL. <safe>It can be exposed.</safe>
oauthClientSecretDesc: Your application's password. It proves to Google the request really came from your server. It must <danger>only ever live on the backend — never in the browser.</danger>
oauthRedirectUriDesc: The URL on your server Google redirects the user to after login. Google only accepts pre-registered URLs — that prevents an attacker from redirecting the code somewhere else.
oauthCodeDesc: A temporary code delivered in the URL after the user authorizes access. Valid for ~10 minutes and single-use — even if someone intercepts the URL, they can't reuse it.
oauthAccessTokenDesc: The credential used to call Google APIs on the user's behalf. Expires in ~1 hour. Always comes from Google after exchanging the code.
oauthRefreshTokenDesc: A long-lived token used to renew the access_token without a new login. It must be kept <danger>only in the database — never in the browser.</danger>
oauthFlowTitle: The full flow — Authorization Code Flow
oauthFlowStep1: Clicks "Sign in with Google" in your app.
oauthFlowStep2: Redirects the browser to accounts.google.com sending: client_id, redirect_uri, scope, and state (CSRF protection).
oauthFlowStep3: Shows the login screen and the consent screen asking whether the user authorizes access to their name and email.
oauthFlowStep5: Redirects back to the app's redirect_uri with a code in the URL. This code is single-use and expires in ~10 minutes.
oauthFlowStep6: Makes a server-to-server call to Google sending the code plus client_secret. This never touches the browser — the client_secret stays protected on the server.
oauthFlowStep7: Responds with an access_token and a refresh_token. Both are generated and signed by Google.
oauthFlowStep8: Uses the access_token to fetch the user's data (name, email, photo) and creates the app's internal session.
oauthServerExchangeTitle: Why is the code exchange done server-to-server?
oauthServerExchangeDesc: Because that exchange requires your application to send the CLIENT_SECRET. If that happened in the browser, anyone opening DevTools would see the secret key. By doing the exchange on the server, the CLIENT_SECRET is never exposed. That's the core reason the flow is called the Authorization Code Flow — the code is just a safe intermediary so the real exchange can happen outside the browser.
oauthDemoWarning: Heads up: this login uses your real Google account via NextAuth strictly for demo purposes. The app only receives the name, email, and photo you authorized — none of it is used for any other purpose.
oauthHowToUseDesc: Authenticate with your Google account using the button below:
oauthStep1Desc: Click the button below to be redirected to Google, authorize access, and return authenticated via NextAuth.
oauthIdTokenPanelHint: The internal structure of the identity token issued by Google
oauthSessionRefreshingDesc: NextAuth is using Google's refresh token to obtain a new access token on the server — without requiring a new login.
oauthSessionExpiredDesc: For demo purposes only — your NextAuth session stays active. In production, NextAuth silently renews the access token on the server without affecting the user.
oauthSessionExpiresIn: The Google ID Token was decoded for display and expires in {seconds, plural, one {# second} other {# seconds}}. Your NextAuth session is managed separately via a cookie and stays active for much longer.
oauthHowSessionWorksTitle: How does NextAuth manage your session?
oauthSessionIdTokenDesc: A JWT signed by Google containing the user's identity claims: sub, email, name, picture. It's shown here only to demonstrate JWT structure — it's not what controls access to the app.
oauthSessionCookieTitle: NextAuth session in an HttpOnly cookie (30 days)
oauthSessionCookieDesc: NextAuth creates its own signed session, stored in an HttpOnly cookie called next-auth.session-token. No JavaScript can read it — that shields it from XSS. Google's tokens live inside it, accessible only on the server.
oauthSessionRefreshTitle: Silent renewal of Google's access token
oauthSessionRefreshDesc: When Google's access_token expires (1 hour), NextAuth uses Google's refresh_token — stored server-side inside the session cookie — to silently get a new access_token, without asking the user to log in.
oauthSessionRestoreDesc: When you reload the page, NextAuth reads the session cookie and restores the authentication state automatically — without redirecting to Google again. The user doesn't even notice.
oauthSessionCountdownDesc: The Google ID Token will display as expired — for demo purposes only. You'll stay authenticated normally, since the NextAuth session is independent and remains valid. In production, the access_token would be renewed automatically by NextAuth on the server.
totpDesc1: TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) is the well-known 6-digit code that changes every 30 seconds from Google Authenticator, Authy, and similar apps. It's the most common second authentication factor on the internet — also called 2FA (Two-Factor Authentication).
totpDesc2: Even if someone steals your password, they still need that temporary code to get into the account. And that code expires in 30 seconds. Without your phone in hand, it's useless.
totpWhyDesc: A password alone is weak. It can be stolen, leaked, or guessed. 2FA adds a second layer: "something you know" plus "something you have".
totpKnowItem1: Your password — can be stolen remotely.
totpKnowItem2: Can leak in security breaches from other services.
totpKnowItem3: Can be guessed via brute force or phishing.
totpHaveItem1: Your phone with the authenticator app — requires physical possession.
totpHaveItem2: An attacker would need to steal the device and use it within the same 30-second window the code is valid.
totpHaveItem3: Works completely offline — no dependency on SMS or email.
totpHowTitle: How does it work in practice?
totpHowDesc: When you turn on 2FA, the site and your phone strike a secret deal. They agree on a shared secret key — known only to the two of them. From then on, both can compute the same 6-digit code at any moment, using that key plus the current time. Since both sides use the same inputs, they arrive at the same result — without ever talking to each other.
totpQrDesc1: When you turn on 2FA, the site shows a QR Code to scan with the app. That QR Code isn't the 6-digit code. It contains the secret key the site and phone are about to share.
totpQrDesc2: Once scanned, the app stores that secret and starts computing codes automatically. If you can't scan it, the site also shows the secret as text for manual entry.
totpBackupDesc1: On activation, the site generates 8 to 10 emergency codes to use if you lose your phone. Each code works exactly once — like boarding passes: use it, and it's gone.
totpBackupDesc2: The site shows these codes only once. It only keeps a scrambled version of them (a hash). If you don't save them, there's no way to recover them. Store them in a password manager or somewhere safe.
totpFlowEnableStep2: The site generates a secret key and shows a QR Code.
totpFlowEnableStep3: You scan the QR Code with the authenticator app.
totpFlowEnableStep4: The app starts showing 6-digit codes every 30 seconds.
totpFlowEnableStep5: You enter the current code to confirm the setup worked.
totpFlowEnableStep6: 2FA is on — save the backup codes.
totpFlowLoginStep1: You enter your email and password as usual.
totpFlowLoginStep2: The site detects 2FA is on and asks for the code.
totpFlowLoginStep3: You open the app and enter the 6-digit code.
totpFlowLoginStep4: The site computes the expected code with the same logic and validates it — access granted.
totpDemoWarning: Heads up: the 2FA activation below is for demo purposes only, tied to the user created in the JWT module. Data is stored in a cloud database and removed by periodic cleanup jobs — none of it is used for any other purpose.
totpDemoHint: The demo moves through three steps
totpStep1Title: Step 1 — Log in to the app
totpStep1Desc: To link 2FA to your account, you need to be authenticated. Log in in the Step 2 — Authentication section of the JWT (JSON Web Token) module.
totpStep2Desc: Click "Enable 2FA", scan the QR Code with Google Authenticator or Authy, and save the backup codes somewhere safe before closing this page.
totpIdleHint: Click "Enable 2FA" to generate your QR Code
totpAlreadyConfiguredDesc: You already have an authenticator app linked to this account. Skip to Step 3 and enter your app's code to validate.
totpOneTimeWarning: This data is shown only once. Save the backup codes before closing this section — they can't be recovered afterward.
totpQrPanelHint: Scan with Google Authenticator, Authy, or similar
totpManualEntryHint: Can't scan it? Enter it manually in the app:
totpDownloadFileFooter2: Store them in a password manager or somewhere safe.
totpStep3Desc: With the app set up, test it live: enter the 6-digit code shown in your app — or use an emergency backup code. The server validates it in real time and returns instant feedback.
totpBackupFormatHint: Format XXXX-XXXX — each code works exactly once
totpValidCodeDesc: The server confirmed the code is correct and in sync with the app.
totpBackupAcceptedDesc: The code was validated and consumed — it can't be reused.
totpReconfigureTitle: Want to reconfigure 2FA on a new phone?
totpReconfigureDesc: Click Reconfigure 2FA to remove the current setup and generate a new QR Code — a new secret and new backup codes will be created from scratch.
totpInvalidCodeDesc: The code expired or doesn't match. Wait for the next 30s cycle and try again.
totpInvalidBackupDesc: This code doesn't exist or has already been used.
webAuthnDesc1: The Web Authentication API (WebAuthn) solves an old problem on the web: passwords are weak, easy to forget, and constantly leak in attacks. With WebAuthn, authentication switches to public-key cryptography — removing the need to create or memorize passwords.
webAuthnDesc2: In practice, it lets users log in with biometrics like a fingerprint or face scan, their device PIN, or physical security keys like a YubiKey — the same mechanism that already unlocks your phone, now used to access web applications.
webAuthnDesc3: It works in two steps. During registration, the device generates a cryptographic key pair: the private key stays protected on the device and never leaves it, while the public key is sent to the server. During authentication, the server sends a random challenge, the device signs it with the private key, and the server verifies the signature — with no password involved at any point.
webAuthnDesc4: Since the private key never travels over the network, there's no password to steal, reuse, or intercept via phishing. Even if the server's database is compromised, the stored public keys are useless without the original device. WebAuthn is part of the FIDO2 standard and is the technology behind Passkeys — the new passwordless login standard adopted by Google, Apple, and Microsoft.
webAuthnDesc5: The result is a much safer and faster login experience: no passwords to type, no phishing risk, and the same ease as unlocking your phone. PWAs and modern web applications that adopt WebAuthn offer a level of security that's hard to match even with the best traditional password managers.
webAuthnQ1: What if a hacker breaks into my device? Can they steal my credentials?
webAuthnA1: The private key is stored in a secure hardware chip — like the Secure Enclave on iPhone or the TPM on Windows PCs. That chip is designed to never export the key, not even to the operating system itself. An attacker could only use the credential on that specific device by defeating your biometrics or PIN — but can't copy the key to use it elsewhere.
webAuthnQ2: I have two different phones on the same account. Is the key the same on both?
webAuthnA2: No. Each device generates its own key pair completely independently. The server stores each device's public key separately, linked to the same account. Each device needs to be registered individually — but if one is lost or compromised, its credentials can be revoked without affecting the others.
webAuthnQ3: If I lend my phone to someone else, can they access my account?
webAuthnA3: It depends on how you registered. If you used a fingerprint, only your finger unlocks access — someone else's fingerprint simply won't work. If you used a PIN and the other person knows it, then yes, they could get in. WebAuthn's security is directly tied to how securely your device itself is locked.
webAuthnQ4: How does the server know who's who when multiple users register different devices?
webAuthnA4: During registration, each device generates a unique identifier called a credential ID and sends it to the server together with the public key, linked to the user's account. During authentication, the device presents that credential ID alongside the challenge signature. The server looks up the public key tied to that credential ID, verifies the signature, and knows exactly which account to grant access to — with no password involved.
webAuthnQ5: Is my fingerprint or face sent to the server?
webAuthnA5: No. Your biometrics never leave your device. When you use a fingerprint or face scan, the device only uses that data to unlock the private key locally. The server never sees, stores, or processes any biometric data — it only receives the cryptographic signature generated by the private key.
webAuthnQ6: What if I lose or break my phone? Do I lose access to my account forever?
webAuthnA6: Not necessarily. Well-built applications let you register multiple devices on the same account — just use another already-registered device to keep accessing it. Many services also offer alternative recovery methods, like email or a backup code. Passkeys synced via iCloud or Google Password Manager even survive a device swap.
webAuthnQ7: What are Passkeys? Are they the same thing as WebAuthn?
webAuthnA7: Passkeys are built on top of WebAuthn and the FIDO2 standard, but add automatic cloud sync — via iCloud for Apple devices, or Google Password Manager on Android. That means your credentials are automatically available across every device in the same ecosystem, solving the lost-device problem. That's why Google, Apple, and Microsoft push the term Passkeys so hard — it's WebAuthn's practical evolution for everyday users.
webAuthnHowToUseDesc: Run the full flow in two steps: register a credential with your username, then validate it using your device's biometrics.
webAuthnStep1Desc: Enter a username and click "Create Passkey". The browser will open the operating system's native prompt asking for your biometrics or PIN.
webAuthnStep2Desc: With a credential registered, click "Authenticate". The browser will show the validation prompt again.
webAuthnUnsupportedMessage: WebAuthn isn't supported in this browser, or this context isn't secure (HTTPS is required).
webAuthnMethod1: navigator.credentials.create(options) — registers a new WebAuthn credential on the device, generating the key pair and prompting the user for biometrics or a PIN
webAuthnMethod2: navigator.credentials.get(options) — authenticates the user by having the device sign a challenge with the registered credential's private key
